text:
My take: the boundary gap is real, and it is not closed by an ordinary source-side cellular deformation. If \(W_y=\partial C_y^0\), then
\[
f_\#W_y=f_\#Z_y=P_y,\qquad N_y:=W_y-Z_y\in \ker f_\# .
\]
If a source chain map \(P_z\) is applied to \(C_y^0\) and a homotopy \(H_z\) to \(Z_y\), the boundary defect is essentially \(f_\#P_zN_y\). There is no reason for this to vanish unless \(P_z\) preserves \(\ker f_\#\). Neither ordinary Federer--Fleming deformation nor a Manhattan carrier has this equivariance. The \(f\)-null difference \(N_y\) can be huge or oscillatory and is not controlled by \(E_y\) or by \(\Mass(C_y^0\cap{\mathcal B}_z)\). So a primal proof using arbitrary near-minimizing essential chains is obstructed.

The clean way I would try to make this rigorous is not to modify \(C_y^0\) pointwise, but to pass to the quotient chain complex
\[
\mathcal Q_k=I_k(R,\partial R)/\ker f_\# .
\]
Then \([W_y]=[Z_y]\), and \(\EFill_f(y)\) is exactly the filling norm of the class \([Z_y]\) in \(\mathcal Q_\ast\). The boundary mismatch disappears formally.

A plausible proof route is then dual/cellular:

1. Cubulate \(R\), pass to finite cellular chains with quotient norm induced from mass, and prove the estimate there. Then pass back by deformation/approximation.

2. Use Hahn--Banach duality for the quotient filling norm. To prove \(\EFill_f(y)\le M_y\), it suffices to bound \(\langle\phi,[Z_y]\rangle\) for every quotient \(2\)-cochain \(\phi\) with \(\|\delta\phi\|\le1\).

3. For each puncture \(z\), construct quotient cochain operators \(K_z,\Pi_z,L_z\) with a homotopy identity
\[
\phi=K_z\delta\phi+\Pi_z\phi+\delta L_z\phi
\]
on \(\mathcal Q^\ast\), where \(\Pi_z\) has coboundary supported only on exact-plaque-bad cells. Pairing with \([Z_y]\), the \(\delta L_z\) term vanishes, the \(K_z\delta\phi\) term should be bounded by
\[
C\bigl(R_1E_y+E_y^2/S_1\bigr),
\]
and the \(\Pi_z\phi\) term is converted using \(\partial[C_y^0]=[Z_y]\):
\[
\langle\Pi_z\phi,[Z_y]\rangle
=\langle\delta\Pi_z\phi,[C_y^0]\rangle
\lesssim \Mass(C_y^0\cap{\mathcal B}_z).
\]
Averaging over \(y,z\) would give EAT.

This is the only route I see that genuinely addresses the boundary issue. But it requires a new quotient-equivariant exact-plaque cochain homotopy. A standard acyclic carrier is insufficient unless it descends to \(\mathcal Q_\ast\), i.e. preserves \(f\)-null chains.

Changing the definition to require \(\partial C=Z_y\) would close the boundary gap but reintroduce the null-sheet/flap problem. Allowing \(\partial C=Z_y+N\), \(f_\#N=0\), is essentially the quotient formulation above, so it helps only if paired with the quotient-dual argument. Thus: EAT may be salvageable as a quotient cochain theorem, but not as a straightforward chain homotopy of the chosen \(C_y^0\).
